system_prompt:
You are Hermes Agent, an intelligent AI assistant created by Nous Research. You are helpful, knowledgeable, and direct. You assist users with a wide range of tasks including answering questions, writing and editing code, analyzing information, creative work, and executing actions via your tools. You communicate clearly, admit uncertainty when appropriate, and prioritize being genuinely useful over being verbose unless otherwise directed below. Be targeted and efficient in your exploration and investigations.

Conversation started: Sunday, April 12, 2026 01:16 AM
Model: anthropic/claude-opus-4.6
Provider: openrouter

You are on a text messaging communication platform, Telegram. Please do not use markdown as it does not render. You can send media files natively: to deliver a file to the user, include MEDIA:/absolute/path/to/file in your response. Images (.png, .jpg, .webp) appear as photos, audio (.ogg) sends as voice bubbles, and videos (.mp4) play inline. You can also include image URLs in markdown format ![alt](url) and they will be sent as native photos.

function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

Replace mode (default): find a unique string and replace it.
Patch mode: apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes.

mode.description: Edit mode: 'replace' for targeted find-and-replace, 'patch' for V4A multi-file patches

path.description: File path to edit (required for 'replace' mode)

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'replace' mode). Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'replace' mode). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
V4A format patch content (required for 'patch' mode). Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: process
function.description: Manage background processes started with terminal(background=true). Actions: 'list' (show all), 'poll' (check status + new output), 'log' (full output with pagination), 'wait' (block until done or timeout), 'kill' (terminate), 'write' (send raw stdin data without newline), 'submit' (send data + Enter, for answering prompts).

action.description: Action to perform on background processes

session_id.description: Process session ID (from terminal background output). Required for all actions except 'list'.

data.description: Text to send to process stdin (for 'write' and 'submit' actions)

timeout.description: Max seconds to block for 'wait' action. Returns partial output on timeout.

offset.description: Line offset for 'log' action (default: last 200 lines)

limit.description: Max lines to return for 'log' action

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: terminal
function.description:
Execute shell commands on a Linux environment. Filesystem usually persists between calls.

Do NOT use cat/head/tail to read files — use read_file instead.
Do NOT use grep/rg/find to search — use search_files instead.
Do NOT use ls to list directories — use search_files(target='files') instead.
Do NOT use sed/awk to edit files — use patch instead.
Do NOT use echo/cat heredoc to create files — use write_file instead.
Reserve terminal for: builds, installs, git, processes, scripts, network, package managers, and anything that needs a shell.

Foreground (default): Commands return INSTANTLY when done, even if the timeout is high. Set timeout=300 for long builds/scripts — you'll still get the result in seconds if it's fast. Prefer foreground for short commands.
Background: Set background=true to get a session_id. Two patterns:
  (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers).
  (2) Long-running tasks with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working on other things and the system auto-notifies you when the task finishes. Great for test suites, builds, deployments, or anything that takes more than a minute.
Use process(action="poll") for progress checks, process(action="wait") to block until done.
Working directory: Use 'workdir' for per-command cwd.
PTY mode: Set pty=true for interactive CLI tools (Codex, Claude Code, Python REPL).

Do NOT use vim/nano/interactive tools without pty=true — they hang without a pseudo-terminal. Pipe git output to cat if it might page.
Important: cloud sandboxes may be cleaned up, idled out, or recreated between turns. Persistent filesystem means files can resume later; it does NOT guarantee a continuously running machine or surviving background processes. Use terminal sandboxes for task work, not durable hosting.

command.description: The command to execute on the VM

background.description: Run the command in the background. Two patterns: (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers). (2) Long-running tasks paired with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working and get notified when the task finishes. For short commands, prefer foreground with a generous timeout instead.

timeout.description: Max seconds to wait (default: 180). Returns INSTANTLY when command finishes — set high for long tasks, you won't wait unnecessarily.

workdir.description: Working directory for this command (absolute path). Defaults to the session working directory.

check_interval.description: Seconds between automatic status checks for background processes (gateway/messaging only, minimum 30). When set, I'll proactively report progress.

pty.description: Run in pseudo-terminal (PTY) mode for interactive CLI tools like Codex, Claude Code, or Python REPL. Only works with local and SSH backends. Default: false.

notify_on_complete.description: When true (and background=true), you'll be automatically notified when the process finishes — no polling needed. Use this for tasks that take a while (tests, builds, deployments) so you can keep working on other things in the meantime.

function.name: write_file
function.description: Write content to a file, completely replacing existing content. Use this instead of echo/cat heredoc in terminal. Creates parent directories automatically. OVERWRITES the entire file — use 'patch' for targeted edits.

path.description: Path to the file to write (will be created if it doesn't exist, overwritten if it does)

content.description: Complete content to write to the file

[Subdirectory context discovered: /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/AGENTS.md]
# Law Firm Wiki — Compiler Schema

> This file is the specification for the LLM that compiles activity logs
> into structured knowledge articles. Read it in full before any compile
> or query operation.

## What You Are

You are a knowledge compiler for a personal injury law firm. Your job is
to read raw case activity logs and extract reusable institutional knowledge
— the process, pitfalls, patterns, and best practices that make a PI
practice work.

You are NOT creating a case database. You are building a **wiki** — a
structured collection of concept articles about HOW PI cases work, derived
from evidence in real cases.

## The Three Operations

### 1. COMPILE (ingest raw → update wiki)

Read activity logs and case files. For each meaningful pattern you identify:

1. Check `index.md` — does a concept article already exist?
2. If yes → UPDATE it: add new evidence, adjust confidence, update Related
3. If no → CREATE it: full article per the Article Schema below
4. If two concepts interact → create/update a Connection article
5. Update `index.md` with any new or modified articles
6. Append a summary to `log.md`

**What to extract:**
- Process patterns (what happens and in what order)
- Decision points (why certain choices were made)
- Problems and resolutions (what went wrong, how it was fixed)
- Timeline patterns (how long things actually take)
- Communication patterns (what works with adjusters, clients, providers)
- Edge cases (unusual situations and how they were handled)
- Anti-patterns (things that caused problems)

**What to IGNORE:**
- Individual client PII (names, SSN, DOB, addresses, phone numbers)
- Specific dollar amounts tied to identifiable cases
- Attorney-client privileged communications
- Raw email addresses and phone numbers
- Replace specifics with anonymized references: "Case A", "Case B", or
  descriptive patterns: "in cases involving [treatment gap > 30 days]"

**Confidence scoring:**
- high: Pattern observed in 5+ cases with consistent outcomes
- medium: Pattern observed in 2-4 cases
- low: Single case observation, or conflicting evidence

### 2. QUERY (ask questions → synthesize answers)

1. Read `index.md` to identify 3-10 relevant concept articles
2. Read those articles in full
3. Synthesize an answer with [[wikilink]] citations
4. If the answer reveals a gap → note it in the answer
5. Optionally file the Q&A back into `wiki/qa/`

### 3. LINT (health check)

Run these checks and write results to `wiki/reports/`:
- **Broken wikilinks**: Links that point to non-existent articles
- **Orphan articles**: Concepts with zero inbound links
- **Phase coverage**: Which PHASE_DAG phases have sparse knowledge
- **Stale articles**: Source data changed since last compile
- **Contradictions**: Articles that contain conflicting guidance

## Article Schema

### Concept Article (wiki/concepts/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Human-Readable Title
slug: kebab-case-slug
phase: [phase_0_onboarding]    # which PHASE_DAG phases this applies to
category: one-of-below
tags: [tag1, tag2, tag3]
confidence: high               # high/medium/low
evidence_count: 5              # number of cases with supporting evidence
created: YYYY-MM-DD
updated: YYYY-MM-DD
related:                       # other concept slugs
  - related-concept-one
  - related-concept-two
---

# Title

> One-paragraph summary (2-3 sentences max)

## Pattern

Detailed explanation of the pattern, process, or concept.

## Evidence

Anonymized observations from source cases:
- "In N cases where [condition], [outcome]"
- "When [action was taken], [result]"

## Agent Guidance

Actionable instructions for paralegal agents:
1. Step one
2. Step two
3. Step three

## Pitfalls

- Common mistake one
- Common mistake two

## Related

- [[related-concept-one]] — how it connects
- [[related-concept-two]] — how it connects
```

### Categories

- `process`: Standard workflows and procedures
- `adjuster-tactics`: Insurance company behavior patterns
- `edge-cases`: Unusual situations requiring special handling
- `phase-knowledge`: Phase-specific best practices
- `communication`: Client/adjuster/provider communication patterns
- `financial`: Billing, liens, settlements, funding
- `medical`: Treatment patterns, records, chronologies
- `legal`: Litigation, discovery, depositions, trial
- `tools`: Software, systems, and process tools

### Connection Article (wiki/connections/*.md)

Same schema but `category: connection` and the body explains how
two or more concepts interact. Example:

```
treatment-gaps-affect-demand-value.md
→ Links: treatment-gap-defense + demand-timing-strategy
→ Insight: Cases with documented gap explanations recovered 80% of
  expected value vs 50% for unexplained gaps
```

### Q&A Article (wiki/qa/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Question text
slug: question-slug
date: YYYY-MM-DD
sources:                       # concept articles consulted
  - concept-slug-one
  - concept-slug-two
---

# Question

[Full question text]

## Answer

[Synthesized answer with [[wikilink]] citations]

## Gaps Identified

[Any knowledge gaps the question revealed]
```

## Index Schema (wiki/index.md)

The master catalog. This is the LLM's entry point for all queries.

```markdown
# Law Firm Wiki — Index

Last compiled: YYYY-MM-DD | Articles: N | Connections: N

## By Phase

### Phase 0: Onboarding
| Article | Category | Confidence | Evidence |
|---------|----------|------------|----------|
| [[concepts/onboarding-pitfalls]] | phase-knowledge | high | 15 |

### Phase 1: File Setup
...

## By Category

### Adjuster Tactics
| Article | Phase | Confidence |
|---------|-------|------------|
| [[concepts/treatment-gap-defense]] | 2,3 | high |
...

## Recent Updates

| Date | Article | Action |
|------|---------|--------|
| 2026-04-12 | treatment-gap-defense | created |
```

## Activity Log Format Reference

Activity logs live at `cases/<slug>/Activity Log/*.md` and have this structure:

```yaml
---
schema_version: 2
case_id: uuid
date: "YYYY-MM-DD"
time: "HH:MM:SS"         # optional
category: legal|correspondence|imported|financial|medical|task
subcategory: email|filevine_negotiation|outbound_call|...
---

# Category — Date

**Case:** [[cases/slug/slug|Client Name]]
**Source:** description

[Free-form content: emails, notes, call summaries, task descriptions]
```

### Subcategory Distribution (21K+ logs)
- email: 3,078
- filevine_negotiation: 1,620
- outbound_call: 890
- missed_call: 780
- filevine_treatment: 717
- filevine_demandinprogress: 645
- demand: 534
- filevine_filesetup: 369
- phase_change: 254
- filevine_uim: 179
- inbound_call: 137
- esignature: 78
- call_summary: 10

## PHASE_DAG Reference

The case lifecycle has 8 phases:
- Phase 0: Onboarding (contract, HIPAA, intake)
- Phase 1: File Setup (insurance claims, provider setup)
- Phase 2: Treatment (monitoring, records collection)
- Phase 3: Demand (drafting, review, sending)
- Phase 4: Negotiation (offers, counters, strategy)
- Phase 5: Settlement (agreement, disbursement)
- Phase 6: Lien Resolution (PIP waterfall, lien negotiation)
- Phase 7: Litigation (complaint, discovery, trial)
- Phase 8: Closed

## Rules

1. NEVER include identifiable client information in wiki articles
2. Anonymize all case references (use "Case A", "Case B", or descriptive patterns)
3. Every article must have at least one [[wikilink]] to a related concept
4. Update index.md whenever you create or modify an article
5. Append to log.md whenever you compile
6. Confidence scores must reflect actual evidence count
7. When in doubt about a pattern's validity, use confidence: low
8. Prefer specific actionable guidance over vague advice
9. Group knowledge by PHASE_DAG phase when possible
10. Flag contradictions explicitly — don't silently resolve them

[Subdirectory context discovered: /opt/data/FirmVault/CLAUDE.md]
# FirmVault — Agent Orientation

> **Read this in full at the start of every session.** It names the thesis, the non-negotiable rules, and where to find the authoritative docs. Short deliberately — use the imports below for detail.

## What this repo is

FirmVault is the **development environment for an AI paralegal backend** intended to eventually be sold to other personal-injury law firms. This specific repo is Whaley Law Firm's own instance; the architecture we prove here becomes the product.

Two parallel product tracks exist:

1. **This repo** — the GitHub-native paralegal backend: markdown vault, `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, `SKILL.md` library, runtime (materializer + worker), intake pipeline.
2. **Separate Huly fork** (not in this repo) — the lawyer-facing UI. This repo's "UI" is for operators, not lawyers.

## The thesis

**"The firm as a code repo."** Law firm work is repeatable tasks + templates + rules, which is exactly what code is. Put the firm's work into primitives agents already excel at (issues, branches, PRs, diffs) and you get a much better AI experience than any traditional PMS bolt-on.

## Core architectural principles

1. **The vault is a shadow, not the source of truth.** Real files (signed PDFs, audio, emails, faxes) live on the firm's own storage (their server, Dropbox, Box, Drive). The markdown in `cases/<slug>/` is a PHI-masked projection of those files.
2. **Intake is a pipeline, not an agent**: document arrives → real file placed on user's storage → converted to markdown → PHI stripped → opens a PR against this repo. The PR is the first human-in-the-loop gate.
3. **Git is the deliberate interface.** Every agent action is a commit or PR. Free audit trail, free approval gates, trivial rollback. Agents *never* touch real files — only their masked shadows.
4. **Markdown, not a graph DB.** We tried FalkorDB and abandoned it. LLM agents are empirically more reliable on plain text + grep than on Cypher queries. This is a deliberate choice, not laziness.
5. **PHI never lives in the vault.** Placeholders like `{{client_ssn}}` and `{{dob}}` reference secrets held in the user's own infrastructure. At render time, deterministic code (not the model) substitutes real values. The model never sees raw PII.
6. **The vault is the only state store for this layer.** No external database. No per-case JSON files. No FalkorDB. No `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`. See `@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md` for the authoritative mapping.

## Do NOT do these things

These are actively deprecated. If you see them in old code or docs, treat them as bugs.

- **Do not reference FalkorDB, Cypher, `graph_client`, `graph_manager`, or `run_cypher_query`.** All removed. Everything reads the vault.
- **Do not reference per-case JSON files** (`overview.json`, `contacts.json`, `insurance.json`, `liens.json`, `expenses.json`, `medical_providers.json`, `case_state.json`). Deleted.
- **Do not reference `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`** or any absolute-path scaffolding. Everything is repo-relative.
- **Do not edit content between `<!-- roscoe-medical-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-medical-end -->` or `<!-- roscoe-insurance-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-insurance-end -->` markers.** Those blocks are owned by the import scripts and will be overwritten on next sync.
- **Do not invent new vault paths.** If the thing you want to write doesn't have a home in `DATA_CONTRACT.md`, update the contract first (same commit), then write. Drift between contract and reality is the bug.
- **Do not create top-level dashboards, reports, or state files outside the vault layout**. Operator views read the vault via GitHub API or git; they don't create parallel state.

## Where the authoritative docs live

Import these at launch — they are the contract, not me paraphrasing:

@DESIGN.md

@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md

@MEMORY.md

Also non-imported but worth knowing:

- `skills.tools.workflows/workflows/PHASE_DAG.yaml` — machine-readable phase definitions, landmarks, and predicate vocabulary. The materializer reads this.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/README.md` — how the materializer and worker agents drive the task queue.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/task_schema.md` — the contract for task issues (labels, frontmatter, lifecycle).
- `Templates/INDEX.md` — the firm template library (73 slugified templates grouped by category).
- `skills.tools.workflows/Skills/*/SKILL.md` — 42 modernized skills in Anthropic Agent Skills format. Exemplars: `lien-management`, `deposition-prep`, `deposition-strategy`, `pip-application`.

## Slug rules (critical for wikilinks)

From `DATA_CONTRACT.md` §4:

- Lowercase
- Apostrophes and curly quotes **stripped** (no replacement char): `K'daya` → `kdaya`
- `&` → `and`
- All other non-alphanumeric runs collapsed to a single hyphen
- Leading/trailing hyphens stripped

Mismatched slugs break wikilinks across the vault. Slug before you write.

## Runtime model (what's being built)

The system is being turned from passive documentation into an active work-driving loop:

```
Vault (cases/*) ← materializer (cron) → GitHub Issues ← worker (event) → Vault
```

- **Materializer** walks open cases, reads each case's `landmarks:` frontmatter + `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, opens a GitHub Issue for every unsatisfied landmark whose task template preconditions are met. Already prototyped as a prompt in `runtime/materializer_prompt.md`.
- **Worker** is triggered by the `status:ready` label on an issue, claims it, loads the named skill, runs it, commits vault changes that reference the task_id, and flips the issue status. Prototyped in `runtime/worker_prompt.md`.
- **Source of truth** for whether a landmark is satisfied is `case.frontmatter.landmarks.<id>` — the materializer checks this first and only falls back to evaluating a predicate if the key is missing.

## Working branch

All in-progress work lives on `claude/import-cases-from-drive-xiQgL`. Do not push directly to `main` without an explicit request.

## When in doubt

- Read `DATA_CONTRACT.md` before writing to the vault.
- Read `PHASE_DAG.yaml` before touching workflow/landmark logic.
- Read `MEMORY.md` to find out what's in flight and what's pending.
- Check the last 3–5 commits on the working branch to see the most recent decisions.

function.name: terminal
function.arguments:
{"command": "for slug in kdaya-austin amanda-tackore maia-ott deanna-scott diamond-williams corey-todd lorraine-robinson brigido-gonzalez-martinez michael-lanier rebecca-lanier brandon-robinson-jr michelle-mcelroy forrest-muss john-tripamer laniaya-wilson prince-ngabonziza gerald-jacobs; do
  dir="/opt/data/FirmVault/cases/$slug/Activity Log"
  files=($(ls "$dir" 2>/dev/null | sort))
  total=${#files[@]}
  echo "========== $slug (total: $total) =========="
  
  # First 8
  for i in $(seq 0 7); do
    if [ $i -lt $total ]; then
      echo "--- FIRST[$i]: ${files[$i]} ---"
      cat "$dir/${files[$i]}"
      echo ""
    fi
  done
  
  # Last 8 (avoid duplicates)
  start=$((total - 8))
  if [ $start -lt 8 ]; then start=8; fi
  for i in $(seq $start $((total-1))); do
    if [ $i -ge 0 ] && [ $i -lt $total ]; then
      echo "--- LAST[$i]: ${files[$i]} ---"
      cat "$dir/${files[$i]}"
      echo ""
    fi
  done
done", "timeout": 120}

   347|- **UIM claims require a separate litigation track**: When BI limits are insufficient, UIM complaints are filed against the client's own insurer in a separate court, with separate discovery and defense counsel

   352|- **Same carrier for multiple claim types creates confusion**: When PIP, BI, or UIM are at the same carrier, different adjusters handle each track — but the firm must carefully distinguish which claim it's communicating about
   353|- **Pre-settlement funding of $31K+ can exceed net recovery**: In complex cases with large liens and extensive treatment, pre-settlement funding can consume most or all of the client's share

    16|
    17|# Intake and Onboarding Workflow
    18|
    19|> The intake process converts a potential client into an active case. It involves collecting personal information, executing the retainer agreement, setting up insurance claims, identifying medical providers, and establishing communication channels. The workflow must be completed rapidly to preserve evidence and begin the claims process.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|Standard intake workflow observed across cases:
    24|
    25|1. **Initial Contact**: Client (or family member) reaches out, usually by phone. Case manager gathers preliminary accident details, injuries, and insurance information.
    26|2. **Information Request**: Firm requests: Name, DOB, SSN, phone number, copy of health insurance card, names/addresses of hospital and PCP.
    27|3. **Contract Execution**: Intake documents sent via VineSign (electronic signature platform) for completion. Documents include fee agreement, HIPAA authorization, and other intake forms.
    28|4. **Welcome Call**: Once docs are signed, firm conducts a welcome call to confirm treatment providers and introduce the client to the process.
    29|5. **Phase Change**: Case moves from Lead/PNC to File Setup.
    30|6. **Insurance Setup**: Send Letters of Representation to both BI (at-fault) and PIP (client's own) carriers. Request acknowledgment.
    31|7. **Provider Setup**: Add all medical providers to the system. Send records/billing requests.
    32|8. **Phase Change**: Case moves from File Setup to Treatment.
    33|
    34|Timeline: This entire workflow typically spans 3-7 days from initial contact.
    35|
    36|## Evidence
    37|
    38|- In Case B, the initial contact came from the client's husband who provided detailed accident information, insurance claim numbers, and adjuster contacts. The firm requested personal info (DOB, SSN, etc.), sent VineSign intake docs, and received signed docs within 3 days.
    39|- In Case C, the intake and welcome call happened on day 1 (4/25/2024). Insurance claims were set up and LORs sent the same day. Records requests went out by 4/29.
    40|- In Case A, intake was complicated by the prior firm transfer — mom signed intake docs via VineSign, but the file transfer from the prior firm took over a week.
    41|- In Case D (chain-reaction MVA), the BI adjuster proactively called the firm looking for the LOR the day after the accident report was filed. LORs were sent to both BI and PIP within 1 day. The provider discovery process was incremental — the client provided some providers on day 1, then additional providers were identified over subsequent calls (TN hospital, TX orthopedic, concussion specialist). This iterative provider discovery extended across weeks. A second accident (occurring one month after the first) was identified during intake, requiring separate claim setup for an NYC cab company (American Transit Insurance).
    42|- In Case F (WC/other case, DOI 12/2022), intake was handled through CaseOpp system. The CaseOpp used the wrong contract type (PI instead of WC), requiring a re-send of WC-specific intake forms. The client's VM was not set up, requiring callback. DocuSign intake docs were viewed multiple times before completion — client reported difficulty with a required field. Despite multiple views, completion took 7 days from first send. Provider identification required a client callback where treatment history was confirmed (ER, orthopedic surgeon, PT).
    43|- In Case G (MVA, DOI 4/2023), intake docs were sent via VineSign on day 1. The welcome call revealed the client had a companion case (daughter) — her file had not been created yet, requiring coordination. Multiple attempts to reach the client failed (VM full), with the firm pivoting to SMS. SSN and health insurance card were not obtained during initial intake and required 3+ follow-up attempts over 2 weeks. The companion case pattern added complexity to intake.
    44|- In Case H (MVA, DOI 12/2022), intake came via a witness/referral (Robert Grant). The firm was unable to reach the client directly — both phone numbers resulted in no answer and no VM. The police report number was obtained from the witness. Eventually the firm reached the client through the witness. This pattern shows that difficult-to-reach clients require alternative contact strategies from the start.
    45|- In Case I (MVA, DOI 1/2024), intake moved from Potential New Client to File Setup to Treatment in a single day. Insurance companies were added, medical providers confirmed via text. The chiro office called proactively about PIP check routing. The client provided provider names via text ("Mary and Elizabeth, Starlite Medical Center"). Rapid same-day intake is possible when the client is responsive.
    46|- In Case J (MVA, DOI 1/2023, UM claim), intake triggered immediate SSN request and welcome call. Client was unreachable on first attempt; VM left. Contact via text/email was initiated same day. Two-week follow-up cycle started. Client eventually responded via text. Provider setup and insurance claims (BI + UM + PIP to same carrier) done within 4 days.
    47|- In Case K (trucking MVA, DOI 2/2024), intake docs were scanned and forwarded by managing attorney. Client phone number was non-functional on first call attempt — staff pivoted to text. Client eventually reached via alternate number. No health insurance. File Setup phase change occurred same day as intake.
    48|- In Case L (MVA, DOI 1/2023, multi-carrier), intake was rapid — records request to hospital sent via ChartSwap on day 1 after intake. LOR sent to both BI carriers (KFB and Progressive) within 24 hours. The client contacted firm about financial hardship (cancelled medical appointments, no transportation) within 6 days — highlighting how intake must set timeline expectations early.
    49|- In Case M (MVA, DOI 8/2024), client was spoken to briefly — she was running errands. She preferred text messages. Firm immediately pivoted to text-based communication. Police report number and health insurance card were requested via text. Records requests sent same day.
    50|- In Case N (WC+BI, DOI 6/2021, transferred from TN attorney), intake was emergency — SOL was the next day. Complaint was filed on the day of intake before any records or provider setup. This case skipped normal onboarding entirely; the firm went straight to litigation.
    51|- In Case O (MVA, DOI 5/2024), intake docs sent via VineSign on day 1. Attorney flagged that 5 years of prior medical records were needed from all providers (not just accident records). This represents a higher documentation requirement for demand preparation.
    52|
    53|## Agent Guidance
    54|
    55|1. Request all necessary information in the first communication (don't spread it across multiple contacts)
    56|2. Send VineSign intake docs within 24 hours of initial contact
    57|3. If docs are not signed within 48 hours, follow up
    58|4. Conduct the welcome call within 24 hours of signed docs
    59|5. Set up insurance claims (LORs to BI and PIP) within 48 hours of signed docs
    60|6. Identify and add all medical providers within the first week
    61|7. Log the phase change from Lead to File Setup once docs are signed
    62|8. For referral cases (husband calling for wife, etc.), get the actual client to sign the docs
    63|
    64|## Pitfalls
    65|
    66|- Slow VineSign turnaround — follow up if not signed within 48 hours
    67|- Playing phone tag with the client for days during intake (use text/email as backup)
    68|- Not capturing all insurance information (both BI and PIP carriers) in the initial contact
    69|- Not asking about all medical providers during the welcome call
    70|- Not sending LORs promptly (this delays adjuster acknowledgment and can delay everything downstream)
    71|
    72|## Related
    73|
    74|- [[case-phase-transitions]] — intake triggers the first phase transitions
    75|- [[client-communication-through-family]] — intake often involves a family member
    76|- [[file-transfer-from-prior-counsel]] — transfer cases have a modified intake workflow

    17|
    18|# Medical Records and Bills Collection
    19|
    20|> Collecting complete medical records and bills from all treatment providers is a prerequisite for demand preparation. This process involves sending HIPAA authorizations, formal record/bill requests via fax and email, and persistent follow-up when providers are slow to respond. Gaps in records collection directly delay case resolution.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|The records collection workflow follows this sequence:
    25|
    26|1. **Provider Identification**: Confirm all treatment providers with the client, including ER, PCP, specialists, imaging
    27|2. **Request Preparation**: Generate HIPAA medical authorization forms and formal records/billing request letters for each provider
    28|3. **Request Delivery**: Send via fax (using RingCentral or similar) and/or email to the provider's records department
    29|4. **Response Tracking**: Track which providers have responded with records vs. bills vs. both
    30|5. **Follow-Up**: If no response within 2-3 weeks, follow up
    31|6. **Client Assistance**: Ask clients to help obtain records from their patient portal (MyChart) when formal requests are delayed
    32|7. **Completeness Check**: Verify all providers are accounted for before moving to demand
    33|
    34|## Evidence
    35|
    36|- In Case B, records/billing requests were sent to St. Elizabeth Florence Hospital via email to a specific contact (Dawn.Probst@stelizabeth.com). The billing info was returned same day. However, getting complete records from Christ Hospital Physicians Primary Care took significantly longer.
    37|- In Case C, records were requested from Baptist Health Louisville via fax on 4/29/2024. Records were received by 5/13/2024 (14 days). A task to "review records and add to Timeline" was created once received.
    38|- In Case B, the client's spouse provided medical visit details from the patient portal (MyChart) while waiting for formal records, which helped move the case forward.
    39|- In Case C, the attorney flagged that records collection had stalled with "no action" for a month, emphasizing that negotiation cannot proceed without complete records.
    40|- In Case D (VA patient), records requests to VA Medical Center Lexington took over 12 months to yield results. Initial request mailed Nov 2023; VA OGC responded Jul 2024. Even after receiving records, they were incomplete — the client reported additional MRIs, physical therapy (VA IPRP program), and injections not in the file. The firm had to ask the client to review records and identify what was missing. The attorney noted: "We're missing a lot of stuff, and that's just part and parcel to the VA."
    41|- In Case D (premises liability, litigation), records had to be requested as CERTIFIED copies for use in litigation — standard copies were insufficient. The certification required correction (date range "04/2014 thru Current"). Records were delivered via Box.com file sharing from ROI quality analysts (e.g., Medicopy). Multiple providers were involved: Grace Health (PCP), Norton/Khalily (orthopedics), CHI St. Joseph (orthopedics + radiology), Baptist Health Corbin, and Corbin Imaging. Records were re-requested to get updated records during active litigation.
    42|- In Case E (auto accident), records review during demand preparation revealed referrals to providers not previously on file (White House Clinics, King's Eye Care). These had to be added and their records requested, delaying demand preparation. Records from one provider (H2 Health) were requested by calling, faxing, and re-faxing over weeks.
    43|- In Case D (20+ providers across 4 states), initial records/bills requests were sent in Feb-Apr 2023, but many providers had not responded by mid-2024, requiring a complete second round of "Certified Medical Records & Billing Request" letters in Aug 2024 — nearly 18 months after the first round. Multiple channels were used simultaneously (email, fax via RingCentral, ChartSwap, EpicLink). Provider confusion arose when billing names didn't match clinical names (e.g., "Norton Leatherman" vs "Norton Neuro & Spine" returned "No Records" despite bills existing).
    44|- In Case F (WC case), records were requested from Medical Center of Scottsville and Riverside Orthopedics via fax within the first week of file setup. The Med Center required a DocuSign-verified authorization (the standard fax copy was rejected), adding a round-trip delay. The provider also needed clarification on scope ("only ER records or all records"). Records arrived piecemeal — radiology CD first, then billing statements separately. A third-party billing entity (TeamHealth) sent bills that were associated with the hospital visit but under a different entity name. Multiple new providers were added over 2+ years as treatment progressed (pain management, shoulder injections, MRI facilities).
    45|- In Case H (MVA, air evac), records collection involved an air ambulance service (Air Evac EMS) requiring fax to a distant location. ChartSwap was used for TriStar Skyline Medical Center records retrieval. The case involved records from providers across multiple states (TN hospital where air-lifted, KY local providers). The air evacuation bill alone was $72K+, making complete billing documentation critical.
    46|- In Case G (MVA, companion cases), records collection was complicated by a companion case (daughter) requiring separate hospital records from Children's Hospital. The client was asked to obtain companion records but failed to do so over months (Oct 2024 to Feb 2025+). Chiropractic records from Southgate Chiropractic were also needed.
    47|- In Case I (MVA), records were requested from UofL Health St. Mary & Elizabeth Hospital and chiropractic provider via fax within the first week. Provider sent records via ChartSwap platform. The PIP adjuster proactively asked about medical records even before the firm's formal requests were complete.
    48|- In a PL case (retail slip-and-fall, 10+ providers), records collection extended over 2+ years. Even during active litigation (July 2025), the firm was still making outbound calls to providers (orthopedics, therapy, PCP) for outstanding records and bills. The medical authorization form was rejected by one provider because it was missing the date of the patient's signature and their DOB — requiring client callback and resubmission, confirming that incomplete authorization forms are a recurring records collection bottleneck. ChartSwap was used as an intermediary for some provider records.
    49|- In an MVA case with companion plaintiffs, certified medical records were requested from a university hospital and chiropractic provider for litigation. A billing error was discovered in the chiropractic records (incorrect certified billing amount) requiring correction correspondence. This confirms that certified records must be audited — providers can certify incorrect billing amounts. Bills from EMS providers were also collected for PIP submission. The case used EvenUP for case valuation, requiring complete records before the evaluation could be generated.
    50|- In Case M (MVA, multi-plaintiff, DOI 8/2023), records were requested from 6 providers including EMS, a university hospital system (separate hospital and physician entities), chiropractic, pharmacy, and pain management. The hospital system had distinct billing entities — hospital bills were requested separately from physician bills, and each was received on different dates (spanning Nov-Dec 2024). The PIP claim was set up same day and PIP info provided to the hospital immediately. The firm also requested and received the police report from the out-of-state police department (New Albany, IN) — the report was not immediately available the day after the accident and required follow-up.
    51|- In Case N (premises liability estate, DOI 7/2020), records collection was complicated by provider misidentification. The firm initially requested records from "CareTenders" (a home health agency), only to discover no records existed — the actual provider was a different home health company. The surviving spouse later provided a calendar from the correct provider, enabling the correct request. Records from one diagnostic imaging provider were requested via ChartSwap, while records from the hospital required a separate LOR to the legal department before records could be released. Bills for the home health provider remained outstanding even 3+ years later during active litigation — the attorney noted during deposition prep that bills were missing. This confirms that provider misidentification is a persistent risk, especially for home health and similar ancillary providers where patients may not know the exact entity name.
    52|
    53|## Agent Guidance
    54|
    55|1. Create a provider checklist during file setup with records requested/received dates for each
    56|2. Send HIPAA authorizations and record requests within the first week of file setup
    57|3. Use both fax and email when possible — some providers respond faster to one channel
    58|4. Set follow-up tasks at 14-day intervals for each outstanding request
    59|5. Encourage clients to share records from patient portals as an interim measure
    60|6. Track records and bills separately — receiving one does not mean you have the other
    61|7. Do not move to demand preparation until ALL providers have been accounted for
    62|8. When the attorney asks about case status, have the records collection status ready
    63|
    64|## Pitfalls
    65|
    66|- Not confirming all providers with the client early (discovering a provider late delays everything)
    67|- Sending requests and not tracking responses
    68|- Not distinguishing between records received and bills received (you need both)
    69|- Letting the client's medical portal records substitute for formal provider records in the demand
    70|- Waiting too long to follow up on non-responsive providers
    71|- Requesting records under a billing entity name that differs from the clinical entity name (causes "no records found" responses)
    72|- Not anticipating that multi-state providers require different request methods and response timelines
    73|

    17|---
    18|
    19|# PIP Claims Management
    20|
    21|> Personal Injury Protection (PIP) is a first-party insurance benefit (from the client's own policy or the at-fault party's policy in KY) that covers medical bills regardless of fault. Managing PIP claims is a critical parallel workflow that runs alongside the BI claim. PIP must be actively directed to pay bills, and its ledger must be tracked for demand preparation.
    22|
    23|## Pattern
    24|
    25|PIP management follows this lifecycle:
    26|
    27|1. **Setup**: Send Letter of Representation to PIP carrier with claim number during file setup
    28|2. **Bill Routing**: As medical bills come in, forward them to PIP with a "direction to pay" (explicit instruction to process)
    29|3. **Ledger Tracking**: Periodically request PIP ledger updates to track what has been paid
    30|4. **Demand Integration**: The PIP ledger is needed before sending the BI demand to understand total medical specials and what remains unpaid
    31|5. **Exhaust Monitoring**: In some cases, PIP benefits may exhaust before all bills are paid
    32|
    33|Key observations from case data:
    34|- PIP carriers may sit on submitted bills for extended periods (30+ days)
    35|- Bills sometimes need to be re-sent when PIP does not process them
    36|- PIP adjusters may respond that they "still have benefits reserved" but have only one bill on file
    37|- Health insurance deductibles interact with PIP: bills may first go through health insurance, with the remainder then submitted to PIP
    38|- Indiana MVAs do NOT have PIP coverage (jurisdiction matters)
    39|
    40|## Evidence
    41|
    42|- In Case C, bills were sent to State Farm PIP on 11/1/2024, but as of 12/31/2024 (60 days later), NO PAYMENTS had been made by PIP. The attorney directed staff to resend and call PIP to identify the issue.
    43|- In Case B, the PIP adjuster from Erie Insurance responded to a ledger request noting "you still have benefits reserved" and only had one bill on file, despite multiple provider bills existing. Additional bills needed to be forwarded with direction to process.
    44|- In Case B, the PIP adjuster explained that one hospital bill was "applied to deductible" by health insurance, which is why PIP issued payment only to the radiology provider.
    45|- In Case A, the case was an Indiana MVA, meaning no PIP was available — this is a jurisdiction-specific distinction.
    46|- In Case D, PIP was used to pay a VA lien directly. The firm sent a "direction to pay" the VA lien ($4,437.13) to the PIP carrier along with the VA billing statement. The PIP adjuster issued payment directly to the Department of Veterans Affairs. This demonstrates PIP's utility beyond paying medical providers — it can satisfy government liens.
    47|- In the same case, the PIP adjuster disputed the relatedness of chiropractic treatment, noting records indicated the treatment was "related to a condition she experienced years prior" and requesting "additional documentation indicating relatedness." PIP adjusters perform their own relatedness analysis and will deny bills they consider unrelated to the accident.
    48|- In Case D (chain-reaction MVA), two separate PIP claims were set up: one with Farmers Insurance (client's own carrier) and one with American Transit Insurance (NYC cab company involved in a separate incident). The PIP application for Farmers was received and returned in early Dec 2022. The case illustrates managing PIP claims across multiple carriers when there are concurrent accidents, requiring careful tracking of which bills are attributable to which accident for PIP purposes.
    49|- In Case G (auto accident, UIM), PIP was managed through Allstate Insurance, the client's own carrier. The PIP adjuster was contacted separately from the UIM adjuster at the same carrier (different adjuster and claim number). The firm requested the ER bill copy from PIP and saved acknowledgment with the PIP log. This demonstrates the pattern where PIP and UIM are both through the same carrier but managed on separate tracks.
    50|- In Case H (auto accident, stolen vehicle), a Kentucky Assigned Claims (KAC) claim was set up through Nationwide PIP. The KAC process applies when the at-fault driver has no insurance (because coverage was denied due to the stolen vehicle). The PIP adjuster from Nationwide (Beth Freeman) managed the KAC claim, which followed a different process than standard PIP — requiring the firm to submit specific documentation and the adjuster to coordinate with other parties regarding billing disputes. The attorney agreed during an EUO that certain bills would not be submitted to KAC for payment, showing that the KAC process involves negotiation about which bills are covered.
    51|- In 13 cases from this batch (all KY MVAs, DOIs 4/2025 to 12/2025), PIP claims were set up with major carriers including State Farm, Progressive, Root Auto, Kentucky Farm Bureau, National General, Elco Insurance, Direct Auto Insurance, and SafeCo. Key patterns confirmed: (1) PIP LOR is sent on day one via fax/email to the PIP carrier; (2) PIP bills are submitted as they are received from providers with the standard request "please process the attached bill"; (3) PIP ledger requests are a recurring task — "please forward an updated PIP ledger" or "please forward a copy of the most recent PIP ledger" was observed in 8+ cases; (4) Same-carrier PIP and BI (e.g., Progressive handling both PIP and BI on separate claim numbers) requires careful claim number tracking; (5) In one case, the paralegal asked the PIP carrier to "process any remaining bills from [provider]" indicating batch bill submission at treatment completion.
    52|- PIP exhaustion was flagged as a concern in one case: "PIP may be exhausted. Sent request for ledger" — confirming that PIP ledger monitoring is critical to understand remaining benefits before demand preparation.
    53|- In a case with an uninsured at-fault driver and no BI available, the attorney directed staff to check for household insurance before setting up KAC PIP — confirming the coverage investigation hierarchy: BI first, then UM/UIM, then household insurance, then KAC as last resort.
    54|
    55|## Agent Guidance
    56|
    57|1. Check jurisdiction first — Indiana MVAs do not have PIP; Kentucky MVAs do
    58|2. During file setup, send LOR to PIP carrier and request acknowledgment
    59|3. As each medical bill is received, forward to PIP with explicit "please process the attached and forward an updated PIP ledger upon completion"
    60|4. Set a 30-day follow-up task for each bill submitted to PIP
    61|5. If no PIP payment is confirmed within 30 days, resend the bill AND call the PIP adjuster
    62|6. Request PIP ledger updates before demand preparation
    63|7. Track which bills have been paid by PIP vs. health insurance vs. unpaid
    64|8. When health insurance pays part of a bill, understand the deductible interaction before submitting to PIP
    65|
    66|## Pitfalls
    67|
    68|- Assuming PIP will automatically process bills — they require explicit direction to pay
    69|- Sending bills once and not following up for months
    70|- Not tracking which bills PIP has vs. doesn't have on file
    71|- Forgetting that out-of-state accidents may not have PIP coverage
    72|- Not understanding the health insurance deductible / PIP interaction
    73|
    74|## Related
    75|
    76|- [[medical-bill-routing-workflow]] — the overall process for routing medical bills
    77|- [[pip-ledger-requests]] — specifically requesting and tracking PIP ledgers
    78|- [[treatment-gap-defense]] — unpaid bills and PIP delays can affect case timeline
    79|", "total_lines": 78, "file_size": 7762, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

     9|created: 2026-04-12

    12|  - medical-records-collection

    14|  - attorney-review-triggers
    15|---
    16|
    17|# Case Phase Transitions
    18|
    19|> PI cases follow a defined lifecycle: Lead -> Potential New Client -> File Setup -> Treatment -> Demand in Progress -> Negotiation -> Settlement. Each transition has specific prerequisites and is triggered by specific events. Understanding these transitions is critical for case management automation.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|Observed phase transitions across cases:
    24|
    25|### Lead -> File Setup (or via Potential New Client)
    26|- **Trigger**: Intake docs signed, client info received
    27|- **Prerequisites**: Signed fee agreement, HIPAA authorization, basic client info
    28|- **Actions on transition**: Create welcome call task, send welcome email, set up insurance claims, begin records requests
    29|- **Timeline**: Same day to a few days after initial contact
    30|
    31|### File Setup -> Treatment
    32|- **Trigger**: All providers confirmed, initial records/bills requests sent
    33|- **Prerequisites**: Insurance claims set up, LOR sent, medical providers identified
    34|- **Actions on transition**: Begin treatment monitoring, send records requests
    35|- **Timeline**: Days after file setup
    36|
    37|### Treatment -> Demand in Progress
    38|- **Trigger**: Client confirms treatment has concluded, or attorney directs
    39|- **Prerequisites**: Treatment complete, preferably all records received
    40|- **Actions on transition**: Confirm treatment status with client, request outstanding records/bills, begin demand preparation
    41|- **Timeline**: Weeks to months after treatment ends (waiting for records)
    42|
    43|### Demand in Progress -> Negotiation
    44|- **Trigger**: Demand package sent to adjuster
    45|- **Prerequisites**: All records/bills received, demand drafted and approved
    46|- **Note**: In transfer cases, may skip directly to Negotiation
    47|
    48|### Negotiation -> Litigation
    49|- **Trigger**: Inadequate offer, SOL approaching, or adjuster intransigence
    50|- **Prerequisites**: Attorney decision to file, complaint drafted
    51|- **Actions on transition**: File complaint, serve defendants, begin discovery; negotiation may continue in parallel
    52|- **Timeline**: Observed at approximately 1 year post-accident when initial offer was very low
    53|
    54|## Evidence
    55|
    56|- In Case A, the case went directly from Lead to File Setup to Negotiation (skipping Treatment and Demand) because the prior firm had already handled treatment and sent a demand.
    57|- In Case B, the transition from Treatment to Demand in Progress was triggered by the attorney noting "no activity since August" and directing staff to check in with the client. Once treatment was confirmed complete, the phase change occurred.
    58|- In Case C, the case went through Lead -> File Setup -> Treatment sequentially, with clear phase change entries in the activity log.
    59|- In Case D, the case progressed through File Setup (July 2023) -> Treatment (July 2023) -> Negotiation. However, the negotiation phase lasted over 2 years due to VA records delays, multiple adjuster reassignments, and incomplete medical documentation. The attorney eventually ordered "prepare and send the demand" in September 2025 (27 months after the accident) despite incomplete records. This illustrates that VA cases can dramatically extend the typical case timeline, and that at some point the attorney must make a judgment call to proceed with available information rather than waiting for perfection.
    60|- In Case D (auto accident, DOI 2/2023), the case followed the full lifecycle: File Setup -> Treatment -> Demand in Progress -> Settlement -> back to Negotiation. The backwards transition from Settlement to Negotiation occurred because the client refused the settlement offer. This shows that phase transitions are not always forward-only.
    61|- In Case E (premises liability, DOI 4/2019), the case skipped the Demand phase entirely and went into litigation (complaint filed, discovery, mediation). PL cases may bypass the standard demand-negotiation flow and proceed directly to suit.
    62|- In Case D (chain-reaction MVA, multiple defendants), the case transitioned from Negotiation to Litigation when the BI adjuster's first offer (~$13K for a concussion/spine case with $60K+ in treatment) was deemed inadequate. A complaint was filed in Jefferson Circuit Court approximately 1 year post-accident. Litigation activities (discovery, depositions, IMEs, mediation summaries) ran in parallel with continued negotiation against a second carrier (Progressive). The case remained in the "negotiation" status in Filevine despite active litigation, revealing that the system status and actual workflow can diverge.
    63|- In Case F (auto accident, dental injury), the case followed: PNC -> File Setup -> Treatment -> Litigation. Treatment was prolonged because the client needed a tooth replacement (dental implant) which extended treatment by over a year. The case moved to litigation without going through a formal demand phase, with a complaint filed approximately 22 months post-accident. This illustrates that dental injury cases can bypass the standard demand phase when treatment extends near the SOL.
    64|- In Case G (auto accident, UIM), the case followed: PNC -> File Setup -> Treatment -> Litigation. A BI claim and a UIM claim ran on parallel tracks. The UIM complaint was filed in a different court (Fayette Circuit) than the BI complaint would have been filed. The case demonstrates that UIM claims require a separate litigation track when the at-fault driver's policy limits are insufficient.
    65|- In Case H (auto accident, stolen vehicle), the case followed: PNC -> Pre-Negotiation -> Demand in Progress -> Litigation. The transition to litigation was forced by the BI carrier's coverage denial (vehicle was reported stolen). The complaint was filed approximately 13 months post-accident. The case then remained in litigation for 3+ years, with discovery, depositions, and multiple defense counsel involved. This is the earliest observed litigation filing, driven by coverage denial rather than inadequate offers.
    66|- In Case G (MVA, companion cases), the case progressed through File Setup -> Treatment -> Demand -> Litigation in approximately 1 year. Complaint filed in Boone County (24-CI-00452). The case went through full litigation including discovery, depositions, and mediation (via Zoom). Mediation failed with last-best-final offers deemed inadequate. This confirms the pattern that filing suit and going through litigation is a common path when pre-suit resolution fails.
    67|- In Case H (MVA, air evac), the case progressed through File Setup -> Treatment -> Demand in Progress -> Litigation. Complaint filed in Barren County (23-CI-00659). The case involved a parallel subrogation action (KFBMIC v. client in District Court). Discovery, depositions, and mediation scheduling all followed. The case remained in "treatment" status in Filevine despite active litigation — another example of system status diverging from actual workflow.
    68|- In Case I (MVA, disputed liability), the case moved from File Setup -> Treatment in a single day. After BI carrier accepted only 25% liability, the firm filed a complaint in Jefferson Circuit Court (~8 months post-accident). The disputed liability was the primary litigation trigger rather than low offers or SOL pressure.
    69|- In Case F (WC case), the phase structure differed from PI: the case involved DWC (Department of Workers' Compensation) proceedings including BRC (Benefit Review Conferences) rather than standard PI phases. This case type uses WC-specific milestones (DWC forms, BRC hearings, IME scheduling through WC process) that map differently to the standard PHASE_DAG.
    70|- In a workers' compensation case (DOI 1/2024), the phase went directly from Potential New Client to Treatment (skipping File Setup), reflecting that WC cases have a different phase structure. The WC carrier accepted the claim and the client was directed to treatment. Deposition scheduling with defense counsel occurred while the case was still in "treatment" phase — confirming that WC cases don't follow the same phase sequence as auto accident PI cases.
    71|- In a litigation case (DOI 1/2021), the case spent 4+ years in litigation: complaint filed in 2021, discovery completed in 2022, depositions scheduled in 2023, motions filed in 2023-2024, and police records collection continuing through late 2024. The case was still in active litigation as of late 2025 with defense counsel communications ongoing. This is the longest observed case timeline, illustrating that complex multi-defendant litigation can extend far beyond the typical 2-3 year PI case lifecycle.
    72|- In an auto accident case (DOI 3/2024), the case was opened in File Setup but was moved directly to "Closing" after investigation revealed no BI or UM coverage (PIP-only through KAC). The phase transition File Setup -> Closing bypasses all intermediate phases. This confirms that case decline is a terminal phase transition that can occur from File Setup without passing through Treatment, Demand, or Negotiation.
    73|- In Case M (MVA, DOI 8/2023), the case progressed rapidly through File Setup -> Treatment (2 days) -> Demand in Progress (5 months) -> Negotiation (same day demand sent, 1/10/2024). A third-party demand tool (EvenUP) was used to prepare the demand, which was saved to the file then edited and sent to both BI carriers. The Demand in Progress -> Negotiation transition was essentially instantaneous once the demand was sent. However, the Negotiation phase stalled for months because the BI policy limits needed to be split among four co-plaintiffs represented by different firms. When inter-counsel distribution negotiations failed, the case transitioned to Litigation (complaint filed in Floyd County). This is a novel litigation trigger: not low offers, not coverage denial, not SOL pressure — but rather failure to agree on policy limits allocation among co-plaintiffs.
    74|- In Case N (premises liability estate, DOI 7/2020), the case went from Pre-Negotiation directly to Demand in Progress on 10/20/2020, just 6 days after the accident report was obtained. The case then moved to litigation (complaint filed in Jefferson Circuit Court 21-CI-003798) and has remained in litigation for 5+ years. This confirms the pattern that premises liability cases frequently bypass extended negotiation and proceed to litigation. The case also involved a referral to outside counsel (Joel Franklin) during the litigation phase, indicating that PL cases against institutional defendants may require specialist litigation counsel.
    75|- In a wrongful death MVA case (DOI 8/2023, KY), the case progressed from PNC -> File Setup -> Treatment status but with the decedent already deceased, the "treatment" status reflected records collection, not active care. The case then moved toward litigation with collision reconstruction experts retained and court filings in both circuit and district courts. This confirms that wrongful death cases challenge the standard phase model — the treatment phase is repurposed as an investigation/records collection phase.
    76|- In an auto accident case (DOI 4/2023, KY), the phase sequence was PNC -> File Setup -> Treatment -> Litigation. The transition to litigation was driven by disputed liability (defense accepting partial fault). The case spent extended time in treatment with 7 providers before moving to litigation. Defense counsel offered 50/50 negotiation during litigation. This confirms that disputed liability remains a primary litigation trigger distinct from low offers or SOL pressure.
    77|- In an auto accident case (DOI 10/2023, KY), the case progressed PNC -> File Setup -> Treatment -> Demand in Progress -> Litigation. The presence of UM and PIP alongside BI created a three-track insurance structure. The transition to litigation occurred when BI coverage was insufficient and UM was needed. This confirms that UM/UIM coverage insufficiency is a distinct litigation trigger.
    78|- In an auto accident case (DOI 9/2021, KY), the case went directly from File Setup to Negotiation (negotiation phase_change logged 6/2023, ~21 months post-DOI), then to Litigation (phase_change 2/2025). The 21-month negotiation phase before litigation is the longest observed negotiation-to-litigation delay, driven by extensive treatment ($176K+ in bills) and multiple adjuster reassignments. This confirms that high-treatment-value cases with policy limits pressure generate extended negotiation before eventual litigation.
    79|- In an auto accident case (DOI 10/2024, KY), the case progressed rapidly through PNC -> File Setup -> Treatment and into Negotiation phase. One BI carrier settled quickly (KFB), while a second BI carrier remained in active negotiation. This is the first observed case where one of multiple BI carriers settles while the other remains in negotiation — confirming that multi-carrier cases can have split-phase statuses.
    80|
    81|## Agent Guidance
    82|
    83|1. Track phase change prerequisites as a checklist
    84|2. Do not advance a phase until all prerequisites are met
    85|3. Log every phase change with the person who authorized it and the date
    86|4. When an attorney asks about a case, report the current phase and what's blocking the next transition
    87|5. For transfer cases, evaluate which phases were already completed by the prior firm
    88|6. The attorney should approve all phase transitions from Demand in Progress onward
    89|
    90|## Pitfalls
    91|
    92|- Advancing to Negotiation without a demand in the file (observed in Case A)
    93|- Not confirming treatment completion with the client before transitioning to Demand
    94|- Letting cases sit in Treatment for months without checking on client status
    95|- Not logging the reason for the phase change
    96|- Prematurely changing to "Settlement" before client has agreed (observed in Case D — had to reverse to Negotiation)
    97|- Staff confusion about current phase when client calls for an update
    98|
    99|## Related
   100|
   101|- [[medical-records-collection]] — records completion is a prerequisite for Demand
   102|- [[demand-timing-strategy]] — timing of the phase transition to Demand matters
   103|- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — certain transitions require attorney review
   104|- [[premises-liability-case-patterns]] — PL cases have a different phase pattern (often skipping demand)
   105|- [[demand-preparation-workflow]] — demand phase entry requires specific prerequisites

   570|### Key New Patterns Identified
   571|- **Union/multi-employer trust benefit plan liens are the largest health insurance liens observed**: A TeamCare union trust lien of $89K+ dwarfs typical private health insurer liens, and trust plan administrative issues (inactive member status) create unique delays
   572|- **Split settlements create multi-track phase ambiguity**: When one BI carrier settles while another remains open, the case is simultaneously resolved and unresolved — Filevine's single-phase model cannot represent this state
   573|- **Technology unreachability during depositions is a new pattern**: Clients willing but technically unable to participate in Zoom depositions require real-time text troubleshooting
   574|- **Ring/busy signal is a distinct unreachability type**: Unlike voicemail-full or disconnected, indefinite ring/busy suggests line issues or call blocking
   575|- **50/50 negotiation proposals occur during litigation**: Defense counsel may propose percentage-based settlement structures as a litigation resolution strategy
   576|- **7 adjusters per case is the new maximum observed**: Multi-track cases (PIP + BI + UM) with parent/subsidiary carrier structures generate the most adjuster churn
   577|- **Wrongful death MVAs may still have extensive hospital treatment**: Unlike instant-death cases, some wrongful death cases involve significant hospital bills and treatment records
   578|- **Dog bite cases naming property LLCs as co-defendants attract sophisticated defense counsel**: Adding the property entity as a defendant can result in major national law firms on the defense side

   805|### Key New Patterns Identified
   806|- **Client at fault as decline trigger**: When the police report shows the firm's client was at fault, the case is declined regardless of injuries or treatment. This is a distinct trigger from coverage gaps — "CLIENT AT FAULT" noted in file, paralegal asks attorney "ok to decline and close?"
   807|- **Incarcerated clients during negotiation**: A client discovered to be in county jail during active negotiation creates settlement authorization barriers. The firm continued case work (PIP ledger requests, adjuster correspondence) but could not obtain client input on offers.
   808|- **Disputed liability stalls negotiation entirely**: In a multi-carrier case, "both sides are denying liability" — the paralegal reported to the attorney "not sure how to proceed." Disputed liability is not just a low-offer problem; it can freeze the entire negotiation process.
   809|- **PIP exhaustion monitoring is critical**: "PIP may be exhausted. Sent request for ledger" — PIP benefits running out before demand preparation creates financial urgency and affects settlement math.
   810|- **Companion batch demand is efficient**: Sending demand for companion cases simultaneously to the same adjuster — "I've attached the records/billing for [companion] as well. Please consider this our demand for policy limits" — reduces duplicate work.
   811|- **Late additional treatment delays demand**: "Sent demands on both but [client] went back for some additional treatment... should be quick to get wrapped up" — treatment that resumes after initial completion requires a second round of records collection.
   812|- **Client wanting in-person attorney meeting**: A pattern distinct from frustration — the client is "difficult. Nice guy but keeps asking to meet with you" — indicating some clients need face-to-face engagement regardless of firm communication quality.
   813|- **Standard 2-week demand follow-up cadence confirmed**: Across multiple cases, the firm follows up with adjusters approximately 2 weeks after sending demand or records to "confirm received" — this is a consistent operational pattern.
   814|- **VineSign intake to family members**: In cases where the primary client is a minor or secondary contact, intake documents may be sent "via vinesign" to a parent or family member, bypassing the client entirely.
   815|- **Same carrier for PIP and BI requires separate claim tracking**: Progressive, Elco, and other carriers handling both PIP and BI on the same case use different claim numbers and different adjusters — requiring careful tracking to avoid cross-contamination of communications.
   816|- **Coverage investigation hierarchy before decline**: Attorney directs: "check for household insurance. If not set up KAC then decline and close" — confirming the investigation order: BI > UM/UIM > household insurance > KAC > decline.
   817|
   818|### Gaps Partially Addressed
   819|- **Demand follow-up cadence**: Now documented at ~2 weeks post-demand across 4+ cases
   820|- **Case decline triggers**: Two new triggers documented (client at fault, no household insurance)
   821|- **PIP exhaustion**: First documented case where PIP exhaustion is suspected
   822|- **Companion batch demand**: First documented pattern of simultaneous companion demand submission

   828|- Rental vehicle liability disputes: One case with rental vehicle at-fault driver dispute, no resolution

   887|### Key New Patterns Identified
   888|- **BI-exhaustion-to-UIM transition is a standard workflow**: When BI settles at policy limits, the COOTS letter → UM claim opening → separate adjuster engagement workflow is now documented from end-to-end in one case. This should be a checklist item.
   889|- **Third-party claims administrator (TPA) demands require adjuster escalation**: When the BI claim is managed by a TPA (CCMSI, Texas Roadhouse self-insured), the demand may need to be re-sent to a different adjuster within the TPA when the first doesn't respond.
   890|- **Disputed liability creates demand-phase stalemates**: When both carriers deny fault and no arbitration has been initiated, the case sits in demand phase with the paralegal unable to proceed. This is a distinct pattern from disputed liability forcing litigation — here the case is stuck BEFORE litigation.
   891|- **Strategy-driven communication avoidance is a new unreachability pattern**: When the firm has no substantive update or strategy for a case, paralegals may avoid calling clients. This manifests as "Not sure what to tell her" and "Not sure what to tell the guy. Any ideas?" — distinct from the client being unreachable.
   892|- **Pre-existing conditions in PL cases add complexity**: A slip-and-fall where the client had prior surgery on the same body part creates a defense argument about pre-existing condition aggravation.
   893|- **Restaurant self-insured BI programs are distinct from traditional insurance**: Claims against restaurant chains may go through the restaurant's own self-insured program rather than a traditional carrier, changing adjuster dynamics.
   894|- **PIP ledger-to-provider coordination is a pre-demand step**: Providers need to know what PIP has paid so they can adjust billing before the demand amount is calculated. This step ("Ledger attached. 7k paid to [provider]. Please resubmit billing") was confirmed across multiple cases.
   895|- **Intraoperative discovery of additional injuries extends treatment**: Surgery may reveal injuries not previously diagnosed (torn rotator cuff discovered during shoulder surgery), extending the treatment timeline and delaying demand.
   896|- **Client preference for no blocked/unknown calls**: At least one client explicitly told the firm not to call from blocked numbers — confirming that client communication preferences should be captured and honored at intake.
