    17|# Companion Case Management
    18|
    19|> When multiple family members are injured in the same accident, each client requires a separate case file but the cases must be coordinated. Companion cases share the same defendant, insurance carriers, and litigation timeline, but each plaintiff has independent medical treatment, damages, and settlement negotiations. The primary client (typically the parent/driver) often serves as the coordinator for all cases.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|1. **Intake**: One family member signs up first; firm discovers additional family members during the welcome call or initial contact
    24|2. **File Creation**: Each companion gets a separate case file with separate intake documents
    25|3. **Coordination**: Parent/primary client is expected to facilitate the companion's document signing, provide their information (SSN, medical providers, insurance)
    26|4. **Records Collection**: Each companion's medical providers must be identified and records requested independently — this often falls on the primary client to facilitate
    27|5. **Litigation**: Companions may be joined in one lawsuit (e.g., "Plaintiff A et al. vs. Defendant") or filed separately. Depositions are scheduled sequentially for companions
    28|6. **Settlement**: Each companion's settlement is evaluated independently, but mediation may address all cases simultaneously
    29|
    30|## Evidence
    31|
    32|- In Case G (MVA), the client's daughter was identified as a companion case during the welcome call. Her Filevine file had not been created yet, requiring coordination. The primary client was asked repeatedly over 6+ months to obtain Children's Hospital records for the companion but failed to do so. Both cases were joined in one lawsuit (Boone 24-CI-00452). During mediation via Zoom, both cases received separate LBF (last best & final) offers. Mediation failed for both.
    33|- In Case I (MVA), a companion witness (Dana Jackson) was deposed alongside the client. Depositions were scheduled sequentially via Zoom — the client was told "we are going to do Dana's deposition first so just chill and I will shoot you a text when we are about done." Coordinating two depositions on the same day required active text communication to manage timing.
    34|- In 2 cases from this batch, companion cases were identified at intake: one MVA had a driver and passenger (mother/son) treated at the same ER, creating parallel files with shared BI carrier but separate demand tracks. The paralegal sent demands referencing both clients' records to the same BI adjuster. In another, a companion case was noted but the intake was sent to the companion "via vinesign" through a family member contact. Companion cases with shared chiropractic providers can have records/billing returned together, requiring manual separation.
    35|- In an MVA case (multi-plaintiff, Floyd County IN), three plaintiffs from the same accident were joined in one lawsuit. The complaint was filed as a multi-plaintiff action. Deposition scheduling required coordination across all plaintiffs through court reporting services. Mediation was being scheduled for all cases simultaneously. The case crossed state lines (KY firm filing in IN), adding jurisdictional complexity for companion case coordination. Two of the settled BI claims were resolved separately per plaintiff, confirming that settlement amounts are independent even in joint litigation.
    36|- In Case M (MVA, multi-plaintiff), three additional passengers in the same vehicle were each represented by different law firms (4 total plaintiffs). The at-fault driver's insurance tendered policy limits (25/50) to be divided among all four plaintiffs. The coordination challenge shifted from records collection to inter-counsel distribution: the firm had to email all three other firms to negotiate allocation. This process stalled for months (Jan-Apr 2024+) as counsel for one co-plaintiff was slow to provide medical bills needed for allocation. The delay frustrated the client, who called repeatedly asking for updates and was told "we are still waiting on a reply on the one person we're waiting for medical bills." When distribution could not be agreed upon, the case moved to litigation with all plaintiffs joined in one lawsuit. Depositions for multiple plaintiffs in the joint suit were coordinated through defense counsel.
    37|- In a bus accident case (DOI 7/2024), two passengers (companions) were injured in the same incident. Both were filed as separate cases but shared the same defendant (commercial transportation company + unknown driver) and the same insurance carrier. Settlement demands were sent for both companions simultaneously. Defense counsel corresponded about both clients in single email threads (\"Abby Sitgraves & Nayram Adadevoh\"). The companion filing was confirmed as \"et al\" in the complaint (Jefferson 25-CI-000133). Each companion had independent medical providers and separate health insurance liens. The out-of-state companion's records collection required different providers than the primary client.
    38|- In an MVA case (DOI 10/2023), the companion relationship was between spouses. The complaint was filed as a companion case in the same jurisdiction. Both clients shared PIP coverage but had separate BI claims. The firm tracked SOL independently for each companion.
    39|- In an MVA case (negotiation phase), a non-English-speaking client had a companion case that was not yet uploaded. The case manager noted they would "wait for the companions case to be uploaded and will make it as the point of contact," indicating that the companion case setup was blocking the primary's file setup. The companion case was referenced by a co-counsel attorney negotiating jointly, with three attorneys (from three firms) coordinating a multi-party negotiation with the same BI carrier. This shows that companion cases can involve inter-firm coordination, not just intra-firm coordination.
    40|- In an MVA case (litigation phase), three passengers from the same vehicle (client plus two companions) were represented by the same firm in a multi-plaintiff action filed in Floyd County. The lawsuit was styled as "et al. v. [Defendant]" and depositions were coordinated across all plaintiffs. Multiple BI carriers and a PIP carrier were involved, with each companion having independent claims against potentially different carriers. The case confirmed that companion case mediation scheduling requires all plaintiffs' availability.
    41|- In an MVA case (closed/settlement), two spouses were injured and filed companion cases against the same BI carrier, with shared PIP and UIM coverage through the same insurer. The firm sent records/billing for both clients to the BI adjuster in the same communication, and followed up on offers for both simultaneously. The BI claim settled first, triggering a COOTS letter, while the UIM track continued separately. This demonstrates the companion case pattern where one claim type settles while others remain open across the same companion set.
    42|- In an MVA case (treatment phase), the case involved 3 separate BI carriers (two at-fault drivers plus a third carrier), with liability accepted by one carrier while negotiations continued with others. The multi-carrier structure meant that each companion's recovery depended on different liability determinations from different carriers, adding complexity beyond typical companion cases that share a single defendant.
    43|
    44|## Agent Guidance
    45|
    46|1. During intake, ask whether other family members were in the vehicle and injured
    47|2. Create separate case files for each companion immediately — do not delay
    48|3. Send intake documents to companions as early as possible
    49|4. Track records collection for each companion independently
    50|5. When the primary client is the gateway to companion information, set explicit deadlines and follow up
    51|6. For litigation, coordinate with the attorney on whether to file jointly or separately
    52|7. Schedule depositions for companions on the same day when possible to minimize coordination burden
    53|8. During mediation, prepare separate settlement evaluations for each companion
    54|
    55|## Pitfalls
    56|
    57|- Discovering companion cases days/weeks after the primary intake (records requests are delayed)
    58|- In Case R (auto accident, DOI 4/2023), the client was identified as a companion case at intake — a note explicitly stated "Companion: [family member]" with a link to the related Filevine project. Both clients were joined in the same lawsuit. Depositions were scheduled separately for both parties at the same defense counsel's office. The companion's case had a distinct demand amount. Both depositions included the defendant, confirming that companion case litigation coordinates plaintiff, companion, and defendant depositions. The case also demonstrates that companion cases may require separate demand evaluations (the companion's demand was for $5K on minor injuries while the related case could have a different amount).
    59|- Relying on the primary client to obtain companion's information without explicit deadlines
    60|- Not creating a separate case file (tracking companion in notes on primary case)
    61|- Not requesting companion's medical records independently (assuming they're covered by the primary request)
    62|- Scheduling conflicts when companions need to attend the same deposition/mediation session
    63|
    64|## Related
    65|
    66|- [[intake-and-onboarding-workflow]] — companion cases are discovered during intake
    67|- [[client-communication-through-family]] — primary client acts as intermediary for companions
    68|- [[litigation-process-patterns]] — companion cases may be joined in one lawsuit
    69|", "total_lines": 68, "file_size": 9961, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    15|---
    16|
    17|# Client Expectations Management
    18|
    19|> PI clients (and their family members) frequently become anxious about case timelines, especially when medical bills go to collections. Proactively managing expectations about the process, timeline, and their role prevents client dissatisfaction and premature settlement pressure.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|Client/family anxiety follows a predictable escalation:
    24|
    25|1. **Early Phase (0-3 months)**: Client is patient, providing information as requested
    26|2. **Mid Phase (3-6 months)**: Client begins asking "Is there anything we need to be doing?" and "Are we close to resolving this?"
    27|3. **Late Phase (6+ months)**: Client receives collection notices and expresses urgency — "I don't want this to hurt our credit" and "We want this to go away"
    28|4. **Critical Point**: Client may pressure for quick settlement at any amount to end the process
    29|
    30|The firm's response pattern is typically reactive: responding to inquiries with brief status updates. Proactive communication is rare in the observed data.
    31|
    32|## Evidence
    33|
    34|- In Case B, the client's husband followed up approximately monthly with increasing urgency. His communications escalated from "Just checking in" (August) to "Do you feel like we are close?" (November) to "We received a letter from a debt collection agency... We want this to go away" (November) to "When can we reach a resolution? We really want to put this to bed" (December).
    35|- In Case A, the client's mother followed up asking "he was asking if I have heard anything about his case" after months of apparent inactivity.
    36|- In Case D, the client left repeated voicemails over a 2-month period (Nov-Dec 2025) that went unanswered, escalating from "trying to find out if there has been an update" to "I have been calling every single day and still have not had a telephone call back." The client explicitly stated "it has been over 3 years" and "I just want everything settled... with whatever" — indicating willingness to accept any amount to end the process. This is the most extreme observed case of client frustration from communication failure.
    37|- In Case D (auto accident, negotiation), the client was told the case was "on settlement phase" and that they "can't disclose the amount yet because it might still change." The client then refused the settlement, creating a confusing situation where staff noted "appears was settled but client didn't accept so maybe ongoing negotiations." This demonstrates the danger of using vague settlement language without specifics.
    38|- In Case E (premises liability, litigation spanning 3+ years), the client texted the attorney directly: "Haven't heard anything from you or an update since court on Monday, and just wanted to reach out." The attorney responded with a substantive update about next steps (records production deadline). This case shows that even in litigation, clients need regular updates after court events.
    39|
    40|## Agent Guidance
    41|
    42|1. Set expectations at intake: explain the typical case timeline (6-18 months for most PI cases)
    43|2. Send proactive status updates at least monthly, even if the update is "waiting for records"
    44|3. When bills go to collections, explain immediately: "We have submitted this to PIP for processing. It may take up to 30 days. The collection activity will be resolved through the settlement."
    45|4. When a client asks "are we close?", give a specific answer: "We need X, Y, and Z before we can send the demand. I expect that to take approximately N weeks."
    46|5. Never leave a client inquiry unanswered for more than 24 hours
    47|6. Document client expectations and concerns in the activity log
    48|
    49|## Pitfalls
    50|
    51|- Being reactive instead of proactive with client communication
    52|- Giving vague responses like "Not at this time" without explaining what the timeline looks like
    53|- Ignoring the emotional impact of collection notices on clients
    54|- Allowing months to pass without any client contact
    55|- Not explaining the process steps that remain before resolution
    56|
    57|## Related
    58|
    59|- [[client-communication-through-family]] — many of these interactions happen through family members
    60|- [[medical-bill-collections-anxiety]] — collections are the primary driver of client urgency
    61|- [[case-phase-transitions]] — understanding the phase helps set realistic expectations
    62|- [[client-frustration-escalation]] — the pattern that emerges when expectations are not managed
    63|- [[negotiation-after-demand]] — negotiation phase requires especially careful expectation management
    64|", "total_lines": 63, "file_size": 4898, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Treatment Gap as Adjuster Defense
     3|slug: treatment-gap-defense
     4|phase: [phase_4_negotiation, phase_3_demand]
     5|category: adjuster-tactics
     6|tags: [treatment-gap, adjuster, negotiation, settlement-value, defense]
     7|confidence: low
     8|evidence_count: 6
     9|created: 2026-04-12
    10|updated: 2026-04-12
    11|related:
    12|  - client-declines-recommended-treatment
    13|  - demand-value-factors
    14|  - pip-claims-management
    15|  - adjuster-reassignment-patterns
    16|---
    17|
    18|# Treatment Gap as Adjuster Defense
    19|
    20|> Insurance adjusters routinely cite gaps in medical treatment as justification for lower settlement offers. A "treatment gap" occurs when a client has a significant period (30+ days) between their initial ER visit or treatment and any follow-up care. This is one of the most common defense tactics in soft-tissue PI cases.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|The adjuster's argument follows a predictable logic: if the client was truly injured, they would have sought consistent follow-up treatment. A gap suggests either (a) the injury was not serious, or (b) the client's current complaints are unrelated to the accident. This defense is particularly effective when:
    25|
    26|- Client only had a single ER visit with no follow-up
    27|- Client was recommended for further treatment (chiropractic, PT) but did not follow through
    28|- The gap spans several months between the accident and any subsequent care
    29|
    30|The treatment gap defense directly reduces the perceived value of the case and is often the primary justification for low initial offers.
    31|
    32|## Evidence
    33|
    34|- In Case A (negotiation phase), the adjuster explicitly cited "the gap in treatment" as justification for the offer amount. The client had treatment at only one provider with no additional follow-up care.
    35|- In Case B (treatment phase), the client's doctor recommended chiropractic care, but the client decided against it due to difficulty coordinating treatment around work and parenting. The client's spouse acknowledged this "might be an area of risk."
    36|- In Case D (negotiation phase), the adjuster cited a related but distinct defense: rather than a treatment gap, the adjuster reviewed additional VA treatment records and found "the claimant never mentioned this accident and all complaints were in reference to a slip and fall" at a different time. The adjuster attributed all treatment to the unrelated incident and made a "top offer" based on that position. This is a variant of the treatment gap defense — the "unrelated treatment attribution" defense — where the adjuster does not deny treatment occurred but argues it was for a different injury.
    37|- In Case F (auto accident, dental injury), the client had a prolonged treatment gap for dental implant surgery — the client was "finally in a position to get his tooth replaced" over a year after the accident (6/2024 for a 4/2023 accident). While this gap was medically justified (waiting for healing before implant placement), it extended the case timeline significantly and delayed the demand/litigation process. The dental treatment gap pattern differs from chiropractic/PT gaps because dental implant procedures have legitimate medical staging requirements.
    38|
    39|## Agent Guidance
    40|
    41|1. During treatment monitoring, flag any client who has not followed up with recommended care within 30 days
    42|2. If a client indicates they are stopping treatment, document the reason clearly in the activity log
    43|3. When preparing the demand, proactively address any treatment gap with an explanation (work constraints, childcare obligations, incarceration, etc.)
    44|4. Advise the attorney when a treatment gap exceeds 30 days so they can counsel the client about the impact on case value
    45|5. In negotiation, prepare counterarguments to the gap defense before the adjuster raises it
    46|
    47|## Pitfalls
    48|
    49|- Failing to document the client's reason for not pursuing recommended treatment
    50|- Not flagging the gap early enough for the attorney to address it with the client
    51|- Allowing the adjuster's gap argument to go unchallenged in negotiation
    52|- Not distinguishing between treatment gaps caused by client choice vs. external circumstances (incarceration, financial constraints, scheduling difficulties)
    53|
    54|## Related
    55|
    56|- [[client-declines-recommended-treatment]] — when clients choose not to follow through on provider recommendations
    57|- [[demand-value-factors]] — treatment continuity affects demand valuation
    58|- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP benefits may still be available to cover follow-up care
    59|", "total_lines": 58, "file_size": 4427, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Unreachable Client Patterns
     3|slug: unreachable-client-patterns
     4|phase: [phase_0_onboarding, phase_1_file_setup, phase_2_treatment]
     5|category: communication
     6|tags: [client-communication, unreachable, voicemail-full, no-contact, phone-tag, text-fallback]
     7|confidence: medium
     8|evidence_count: 7
     9|created: 2026-04-12
    10|updated: 2026-04-12
    11|related:
    12|  - client-frustration-escalation
    13|  - client-expectations-management
    14|  - intake-and-onboarding-workflow
    15|  - case-decline-and-closing-workflow
    16|---
    17|
    18|# Unreachable Client Patterns
    19|
    20|> Some PI clients are persistently difficult to reach — their voicemail is full, they don't return calls, and emails go unanswered. This pattern is distinct from client frustration (where the client is actively trying to reach the firm). Unreachable clients create a bottleneck because the firm needs their input to proceed with intake, treatment confirmation, and case management. Persistent multi-channel contact attempts are required, sometimes over weeks.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|### Common Unreachability Signals
    25|1. **Voicemail full**: The client's voicemail box is full, preventing the firm from leaving a message
    26|2. **No answer, no callback**: Multiple outbound calls with no answer and no return call
    27|3. **Phone number changes**: Client changes phone numbers without notifying the firm
    28|4. **Email unresponsive**: Welcome emails and information requests go unanswered
    29|
    30|### Multi-Channel Escalation
    31|The firm follows a predictable escalation pattern when a client is unreachable:
    32|1. **Day 1**: Outbound call → no answer / voicemail full
    33|2. **Day 1**: Text message sent as fallback
    34|3. **Day 2-3**: Second outbound call attempt + email
    35|4. **Week 2**: Follow-up task created ("Two Week follow up with client")
    36|5. **Week 3-4**: Additional call attempts, emails, texts
    37|6. **Ongoing**: "Still unreachable" logged in case notes
    38|7. **Eventually**: Client responds via a different channel or new phone number
    39|
    40|### Impact on Case Timeline
    41|- Intake tasks cannot be completed without client input (providers list, insurance card, SSN)
    42|- File Setup stalls — the case sits in File Setup for months instead of progressing
    43|- Medical records requests are delayed because providers aren't confirmed
    44|- The case may ultimately be declined partly due to the difficulty of working it without client cooperation
    45|
    46|### Client Eventually Resurfaces
    47|Unreachable clients often do eventually make contact, sometimes with a new phone number. When they do:
    48|- They expect the case to be progressing despite their absence
    49|- They may provide the requested information belatedly
    50|- The firm must quickly re-engage and catch up on delayed tasks
    51|
    52|## Evidence
    53|
    54|- In an auto accident case (DOI 3/2024), the client was unreachable from day one of file setup. Multiple calls were made with the note "No answer, vm is full." Text messages were sent as fallback. The client eventually responded via text ("I will call you later, thanks") and then called back from a different number. The voicemail transcription showed they had also been trying to reach the firm: "We have missed each other for the last day or 2." Despite repeated outreach attempts over weeks, the client remained difficult to reach throughout the case. By 2/2025, the note still read "Still unreachable." The client's phone number changed at least once during the case.
    55|- In the same case, even when the firm was trying to close/decline the case, a staff member noted they needed to speak with the client about the case update — and had to use a different phone number than the one on file.
    56|- In a PL case (DOI 9/2023) in active litigation, the client called the attorney repeatedly (20+ missed calls over 7 months), but the calls went unreturned for extended periods. The attorney then made periodic outbound calls. This is a reverse unreachability pattern — the firm is unreachable to the client rather than vice versa, likely due to attorney bandwidth in a litigation-heavy caseload.
    57|- In an elderly MVA case (DOI 1/2022) in litigation, the client's voicemail was explicitly noted as "full" when the firm attempted to call with case updates. The client called back repeatedly but calls were not answered. This voicemail-full pattern prevents both parties from leaving messages, creating a complete communication breakdown.
    58|- In an auto accident case (DOI 4/2023, KY) in active litigation, the firm attempted to call the client multiple times but the phone "just rings and then gives busy signal." The firm logged multiple attempts: "attempted to contact client, just keeps ringing" and "attempted to call client, just rings and gives busy signal." The client was eventually reached for a substantive conversation about the case status and defense counsel's settlement proposal. This is a distinct signal type — not voicemail full, not disconnected, but an indefinite ring/busy pattern suggesting a phone line issue or call blocking.
    59|- In an auto accident case (DOI 1/2024, KY) during a scheduled Zoom deposition, the firm had to text the client urgently: "Dana you good? We lost you" and "They were having some technical problems. Go ahead and log back on." The client was also texted earlier: "Just a reminder, the deposition starts in about an hour" and "if you use zoom before are you good with using that?" This shows a new unreachability pattern: technology-related unreachability during critical legal proceedings (depositions), where the client is willing but technically unable to participate, requiring real-time troubleshooting via text.
    60|- In a trucking MVA case (DOI 2/2024), the client's phone number was "not in service" when the firm tried to reach her during intake. A text was sent and a second number was tried. The client was eventually reached via an alternate number. This was a pattern of phone service disruption rather than willful non-response.
    61|- In an MVA case (DOI 1/2023), the client called the firm repeatedly but no one answered — calls were logged as "missed" to multiple staff members. The client also texted: "Is your office open today?" The firm's unreachability to the client (many missed inbound calls over months) is the reverse pattern, creating friction and frustration.
    62|- In an MVA case (DOI 1/2023), the client was initially unreachable — "call went to VM - left msg." The client then used an alternate phone number for subsequent contact. New phone numbers should always be captured immediately.
    63|- In a treatment-phase MVA case (DOI 8/2024), the client preferred text over phone calls. When staff called, the client was unavailable ("on an errand"). Text became the primary channel. Confirming preferred communication channel at intake prevents unnecessary phone-tag loops.
    64|- In a demand-phase case (DOI 12/2025), the client's voicemail was full when the firm tried to call with a case update. The paralegal texted instead: "your voicemail is full so I couldn't leave a message." Client had called multiple times in the days prior (multiple missed calls logged) but the firm couldn't return calls. This is the classic bidirectional unreachability pattern — both sides trying to reach each other but missing.
    65|- In a file-setup MVA case (DOI 8/2025), the client called multiple times (missed calls logged weeks apart) but received no callbacks. The case was in demand preparation but the client had no updates. The paralegal was uncertain what to tell the client — a pattern where the firm's inability to provide substantive updates compounds the communication gap.
    66|- In an MVA case (DOI 9/2025) with disputed liability, both insurance carriers were denying fault. The paralegal escalated to the attorney multiple times ("Not sure what to tell the guy. Any ideas?") but received no response documented in the logs. The client's inability to get a substantive update — combined with the firm's uncertainty about the case strategy — represents a new pattern: strategy-driven unreachability where the firm avoids contact because it lacks a clear answer.
    67|- In a file-setup MVA case (DOI 9/2024, KY), the initial contact attempt to the client went to voicemail which was full. The paralegal noted "Went straight to vm. VM is full." The client's boyfriend (a companion/passenger in the same accident) had initially called in. Multiple attempts were needed before the client could be reached for a welcome call. The pattern of VM-full on day one of file setup was confirmed in 3 additional cases.
    68|- In an MVA case (DOI mid-2025, KY), the client called the firm's main line (missed calls were logged to the attorney) but the firm did not return calls promptly. The missed call logs showed repeated attempts weeks apart. This reverse unreachability pattern — where the client is available but the firm is not — was confirmed across 4+ cases in this batch, particularly with smaller or early-stage cases that may not receive priority callback attention.
    69|- In a case (DOI mid-2025, KY), the client was described as "difficult" and repeatedly asked to meet with the attorney in person. The paralegal communicated to the attorney: "[Client] is difficult. Nice guy but keeps asking to meet with you." This is a distinct communication pattern — not unreachability per se, but a client whose communication needs exceed the standard phone/text model, requiring in-person attorney engagement.
    70|
    71|## Agent Guidance
    72|
    73|1. If the first call goes to voicemail and VM is full, immediately send a text message
    74|2. If no response to text, send an email the same day
    75|3. Set a 2-week follow-up task automatically on every case where the client has not responded
    76|4. Log EVERY contact attempt (call, text, email) with outcome (answered, VM full, no answer, etc.)
    77|5. After 3 failed contact attempts, escalate to the attorney with a note about unreachability
    78|6. Always ask the client for a secondary contact number and an emergency contact
    79|7. When the client does respond, immediately gather all needed information in that same conversation — you may not get another chance soon
    80|8. Record the client's updated phone number in the case system immediately when it changes
    81|9. Consider whether unreachability is a pattern that makes the case unworkable — flag for attorney review
    82|
    83|## Pitfalls
    84|
    85|- Only trying to call and not using text/email as fallback
    86|- Not logging failed contact attempts (creates the appearance of no effort)
    87|- Waiting weeks between contact attempts instead of escalating channels
    88|- Not asking for a secondary contact number at intake
    89|- Not updating the phone number in the system when the client calls from a new number
    90|- Assuming unreachability means the client is uninterested — they may be dealing with medical/life issues
    91|
    92|## Related
    93|
    94|- [[client-frustration-escalation]] — opposite pattern where the client is trying to reach the firm
    95|- [[client-expectations-management]] — unreachable clients may still have expectations about case progress
    96|- [[intake-and-onboarding-workflow]] — unreachability most critically impacts intake tasks
    97|- [[case-decline-and-closing-workflow]] — persistent unreachability may contribute to case decline decisions
    98|", "total_lines": 97, "file_size": 11056, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"success": true, "diff": "--- a//opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/intake-and-onboarding-workflow.md
+++ b//opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/intake-and-onboarding-workflow.md
@@ -49,6 +49,7 @@
 - In Case M (MVA, DOI 8/2024), client was spoken to briefly — she was running errands. She preferred text messages. Firm immediately pivoted to text-based communication. Police report number and health insurance card were requested via text. Records requests sent same day.
 - In Case N (WC+BI, DOI 6/2021, transferred from TN attorney), intake was emergency — SOL was the next day. Complaint was filed on the day of intake before any records or provider setup. This case skipped normal onboarding entirely; the firm went straight to litigation.
 - In Case O (MVA, DOI 5/2024), intake docs sent via VineSign on day 1. Attorney flagged that 5 years of prior medical records were needed from all providers (not just accident records). This represents a higher documentation requirement for demand preparation.
+- Across 17 additional small cases (all KY, DOIs 5/2025-2/2026), the standard VineSign intake workflow was confirmed in every case: intake sent via VineSign, signed within 1-3 days, followed by welcome call/text, then phase change to File Setup. In one slip-and-fall case, intake was processed alongside insurance claim setup within the same week. In 2 cases, the welcome call task was auto-generated ("Contact [Client] and do Welcome Call and send Welcome Email(if able). Confirm treatment providers and enter any in system that have not been entered.") — confirming this is a standardized template task. One case involved a client with only a minor-care-clinic visit (single $99 bill), showing that even minimal-treatment cases follow the same intake workflow. In a case involving a Spanish-named client from an out-of-state accident (Indiana), the intake included a rental car agreement forwarded to the file, showing clients proactively providing documentation. In 3 cases, companion cases (same-accident family members) shared intake timing: two companions from the same MVA had overlapping file setup and insurance claim creation on the same day.

The firm initially requested records from \"CareTenders\" (a home health agency), only to discover no records existed — the actual provider was a different home health company. The surviving spouse later provided a calendar from the correct provider, enabling the correct request. Records from one diagnostic imaging provider were requested via ChartSwap, while records from the hospital required a separate LOR to the legal department before records could be released. This confirms that provider misidentification is a persistent risk, especially for home health and similar ancillary providers where patients may not know the exact entity name.\n- Across 17 additional small cases (all KY, DOIs 5/2025-2/2026), records collection patterns confirmed existing workflow: (1) records/billing requests sent via fax and email within the first 1-2 weeks of file setup; (2) requests directed to university hospital systems (UofL Health), community hospitals, EMS providers, and chiropractic offices; (3) UofL Health records returned via ROI department with confirmation (\"Received\" from ROI tech); (4) hospital records and bills sometimes received on separate dates (e.g., records on day 9, bills on day 10 from same provider); (5) EMS records required separate fax request to EMS provider (Louisville Metro EMS, Anchorage EMS); (6) one case had only a single minimal-treatment provider (walk-in clinic, $99 bill) — records and bills were received together within 9 days; (7) in a slip-and-fall case managed by a third-party claims administrator, records from a chiropractic provider revealed that treatment was related to a prior MVA and not the slip-and-fall incident — creating a treatment-relatedness dispute; (8) PIP carriers (State Farm, Shelter Insurance) were sent records/billing requests in parallel with provider requests, with the standard language \"please forward an updated PIP ledger along with copies of any ER related records/bills received to date\"; (9) in 3 companion cases, records were collected independently for each client even when they shared the same accident and some of the same providers."}

A billing error was discovered in the chiropractic records (incorrect certified billing amount) requiring correction correspondence. This confirms that certified records must be audited — providers can certify incorrect billing amounts. Bills from EMS providers were also collected for PIP submission. The case used EvenUP for case valuation, requiring complete records before the evaluation could be generated.

The hospital system had distinct billing entities — hospital bills were requested separately from physician bills, and each was received on different dates (spanning Nov-Dec 2024). The PIP claim was set up same day and PIP info provided to the hospital immediately. The firm also requested and received the police report from the out-of-state police department (New Albany, IN) — the report was not immediately available the day after the accident and required follow-up.
 - In Case N (premises liability estate, DOI 7/2020), records collection was complicated by provider misidentification. The firm initially requested records from "CareTenders" (a home health agency), only to discover no records existed — the actual provider was a different home health company. The surviving spouse later provided a calendar from the correct provider, enabling the correct request. Records from one diagnostic imaging provider were requested via ChartSwap, while records from the hospital required a separate LOR to the legal department before records could be released. Bills for the home health provider remained outstanding even 3+ years later during active litigation — the attorney noted during deposition prep that bills were missing. This confirms that provider misidentification is a persistent risk, especially for home health and similar ancillary providers where patients may not know the exact entity name.

Key confirmations: (1) PIP LOR and ledger requests sent on day one of file setup; (2) standard request language: \"please forward an updated PIP ledger along with copies of any ER related records/bills received to date\"; (3) PIP ledger requests sent to carrier email/fax within the first 2 weeks; (4) in one case, the paralegal requested PIP carrier \"remove any reservation for our client and process any remaining billing that is NOT ER related treatment\" — confirming that PIP carriers hold reservations on benefits and the firm must direct which bills to process; (5) in a companion case pair, both companions shared the same PIP carrier and claim number (Kentucky Farm Bureau #8069631) with the same adjuster handling both; (6) Erie Insurance PIP was set up for a case with UM through Esurance — confirming that PIP and UM can be through related but distinct carrier entities; (7) one case with PIP-only (no BI identified) confirmed the coverage investigation pattern where PIP is the only available benefit."}

@@ -51,6 +51,7 @@
 - In 13 cases from this batch (all KY MVAs, DOIs 4/2025 to 12/2025), PIP claims were set up with major carriers including State Farm, Progressive, Root Auto, Kentucky Farm Bureau, National General, Elco Insurance, Direct Auto Insurance, and SafeCo. Key patterns confirmed: (1) PIP LOR is sent on day one via fax/email to the PIP carrier; (2) PIP bills are submitted as they are received from providers with the standard request "please process the attached bill"; (3) PIP ledger requests are a recurring task — "please forward an updated PIP ledger" or "please forward a copy of the most recent PIP ledger" was observed in 8+ cases; (4) Same-carrier PIP and BI (e.g., Progressive handling both PIP and BI on separate claim numbers) requires careful claim number tracking; (5) In one case, the paralegal asked the PIP carrier to "process any remaining bills from [provider]" indicating batch bill submission at treatment completion.
 - PIP exhaustion was flagged as a concern in one case: "PIP may be exhausted. Sent request for ledger" — confirming that PIP ledger monitoring is critical to understand remaining benefits before demand preparation.
 - In a case with an uninsured at-fault driver and no BI available, the attorney directed staff to check for household insurance before setting up KAC PIP — confirming the coverage investigation hierarchy: BI first, then UM/UIM, then household insurance, then KAC as last resort.
+- Across 17 additional small cases (all KY, DOIs 5/2025-2/2026), PIP management patterns confirmed across 11 carriers: State Farm (4 cases), Kentucky Farm Bureau (3 cases), Progressive (2 cases), National General (1), Erie Insurance (1), Travelers (1), Shelter Insurance (1), Elco Insurance (1). Key confirmations: (1) PIP LOR and ledger requests sent on day one of file setup; (2) standard request language: "please forward an updated PIP ledger along with copies of any ER related records/bills received to date"; (3) PIP ledger requests sent to carrier email/fax within the first 2 weeks; (4) in one case, the paralegal requested PIP carrier "remove any reservation for our client and process any remaining billing that is NOT ER related treatment" — confirming that PIP carriers hold reservations on benefits and the firm must direct which bills to process; (5) in a companion case pair, both companions shared the same PIP carrier and claim number (Kentucky Farm Bureau #8069631) with the same adjuster handling both; (6) Erie Insurance PIP was set up for a case with UM through Esurance — confirming that PIP and UM can be through related but distinct carrier entities; (7) one case with PIP-only (no BI identified) confirmed the coverage investigation pattern where PIP is the only available benefit.

{"command": "cat >> /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/log.md << 'LOGEOF'

## 2026-04-12 — Batch 8 Compilation (Final Small Cases: kdaya-austin, amanda-tackore, maia-ott, deanna-scott, diamond-williams, corey-todd, lorraine-robinson, brigido-gonzalez-martinez, michael-lanier, rebecca-lanier, brandon-robinson-jr, michelle-mcelroy, forrest-muss, john-tripamer, laniaya-wilson, prince-ngabonziza, gerald-jacobs)

**Compiler**: Wiki Compiler Agent (subagent)
**Cases Processed**: 17 (cumulative: 60+)
**Source Data**:
- 17 very small KY cases (DOIs ranging from 5/2025 to 2/2026), mostly early-stage auto accidents with 17-32 activity logs each
- Case types: 16 auto accidents (MVA), 1 slip-and-fall (premises liability)
- Case statuses: 7 treatment, 5 file_setup, 3 demand, 2 other
- All Kentucky jurisdiction
- Key carriers: Progressive (5 cases), State Farm (4 cases), Kentucky Farm Bureau (3 cases), Allstate (2), Erie/Esurance (1), Travelers (1), Shelter (1), National General (1), Elco (1), North American Risk Services (1), Sedgwick (1)
- 3 companion case pairs identified: two MVA companions (DOI 12/2025) sharing BI carrier (Progressive) and PIP carrier (Kentucky Farm Bureau, same claim #8069631); two MVA companions (DOI 6/2025) sharing same BI carrier
- Notable patterns: slip-and-fall with third-party claims administrator (Sedgwick), UM claim through Esurance/Erie, out-of-state accident (Indiana) with minimal treatment, client contact through girlfriend, provider treatment-relatedness dispute

**Articles Created**: 0 (all patterns matched existing articles)
**Articles Updated**: 17 existing concept articles (evidence_count incremented)

### Concepts Updated (with evidence count changes)
1. `intake-and-onboarding-workflow` — 32->49, high (added 17 cases confirming standard VineSign intake, welcome call template task, companion simultaneous file setup, minimal-treatment intake, out-of-state accident rental car documentation)
2. `case-phase-transitions` — 37->54, high (added 17 cases: PNC->File Setup->Treatment standard flow confirmed, demand-status PL case with clear liability following normal demand phase, companion parallel phase transitions)
3. `medical-records-collection` — 24->41, high (added 17 cases: UofL Health ROI department workflow, hospital/bills separate dates, EMS separate requests, minimal-treatment single-provider records, chiropractic treatment-relatedness dispute, PIP carrier parallel records requests)
4. `pip-claims-management` — 27->44, high (added 17 cases across 11 PIP carriers, PIP reservation removal request pattern, shared PIP claim number across companions, Erie PIP with Esurance UM, PIP-only case)
5. `treatment-gap-defense` — 6->8, confidence: low->medium (added provider-confirmed treatment-relatedness dispute from slip-and-fall case, limited single-provider treatment vulnerability)
6. `companion-case-management` — 8->11, medium (added 3 companion pairs with shared carriers and simultaneous file setup)
7. `client-expectations-management` — 17->20, high (added early-stage client inquiry patterns from 3 cases)
8. `unreachable-client-patterns` — 7->10, medium (added girlfriend-as-contact pattern, no-police-report-no-at-fault-info pattern)
9. `client-communication-through-family` — 14->16, high (added girlfriend contact for client, family member phone number provided for welcome call)
10. `uim-claim-management` — 8->9, medium (added UM through Esurance/Erie Insurance Group with separate PIP adjuster)
11. `multi-carrier-multi-defendant-cases` — 12->14, high (added BI+PIP+UIM three-track case, companion cases with shared carriers)
12. `demand-preparation-workflow` — 21->24, high (added 3 demand-status cases: slip-and-fall with Sedgwick TPA, auto accident with all records/bills received, treatment complete gating demand)
13. `premises-liability-case-patterns` — 6->7, medium (added slip-and-fall with Sedgwick TPA following standard demand phase when liability is clear)
14. `adjuster-unreturned-calls-pattern` — 11->13, high (added adjuster liability investigation inquiry, PIP acknowledgment request pattern)
15. `statute-of-limitations-monitoring` — 15->17, high (added 2 new early-stage cases with standard countdown tracking)
16. `attorney-review-triggers` — 12->14, high (added adjuster liability inquiry forwarded to attorney, treatment-relatedness dispute requiring attorney guidance)
17. `medical-bill-collections-anxiety` — 8->11, medium (added early-stage bill concerns from 3 cases)

### Confidence Upgrades (1 article)
- `treatment-gap-defense`: low -> medium (8 cases — provider-confirmed treatment attribution is a new and stronger variant)

### Key Patterns Confirmed (Not New — Strengthening Existing)
- **VineSign intake is universal**: All 17 cases used VineSign for intake documents, confirming this is the standard regardless of case type, complexity, or client demographics
- **Welcome call template is standardized**: The exact same task text ("Contact [Client] at [phone] and do Welcome Call and send Welcome Email(if able). Confirm treatment providers and enter any in system that have not been entered.") was observed across multiple cases — this is clearly a Filevine automated template
- **PIP ledger request language is standardized**: "Please forward an updated PIP ledger along with copies of any ER related records/bills received to date" — observed verbatim across multiple cases with different PIP carriers
- **Companion cases share PIP claim numbers**: Two companions from the same MVA (DOI 12/2025) shared Kentucky Farm Bureau PIP claim #8069631 with the same adjuster — confirming PIP carriers treat companions as one household claim
- **Early-stage cases are operationally identical**: Whether the case involves $99 in medical treatment or $20K+, the intake, file setup, and records collection workflows are the same
- **Slip-and-fall with third-party claims administrator can follow standard demand workflow**: Unlike PL cases against commercial entities that go directly to litigation, PL cases with TPAs (Sedgwick) handling the BI claim can follow the standard demand preparation path

### New Variant Identified
- **Provider-confirmed treatment-relatedness dispute**: In the slip-and-fall case, the chiropractic provider itself called the firm to report that "all the treatment client had there was related to a previous MVA and not the slip and fall." This is stronger than the adjuster-initiated unrelated-treatment-attribution defense documented previously — when the treating provider confirms the attribution, the adjuster's position becomes nearly unassailable. This should trigger immediate attorney review.

### Gaps — No New Gaps from This Batch
These 17 cases were too early-stage to reveal new gaps. All existing gaps remain as documented in prior log entries.
